wiki_catalogs.name: Project Overview
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content for the Nebula project overview section focused on introducing the workflow automation engine. Explain its purpose as a modular, type-safe Rust-based automation platform, design philosophy emphasizing security and composability, and relationship to competitors like n8n, Zapier, and Temporal. Document the layered architecture approach, core design principles (types over tests, explicit over magic, security by default), and the project's current alpha stage status. Include both conceptual overviews for beginners understanding workflow automation concepts and technical details for experienced developers evaluating the Rust ecosystem fit. Use terminology consistent with the codebase including terms like 'DAG-based', 'composable library', and 'production credentials'. Provide practical examples demonstrating the modular nature and security-first approach. Document the crate map showing the seven-layer architecture and highlight key differentiators from other automation platforms.

wiki_catalogs.name: Layered Architecture Design
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create architectural documentation for Nebula's seven-layered architecture. Describe the systematic layering from Core (ID management, validation, parameter system) through Business (credential management, resource pooling, action framework), Execution (engine orchestration, runtime scheduling, storage persistence), API/Public (REST server, integration SDK), and Cross-cutting concerns (error handling, resilience, logging, observability). Document the one-way dependency enforcement between layers, explaining why upward dependencies are prohibited and how cargo deny configuration maintains architectural integrity. Include detailed component breakdowns showing how each layer contributes to the overall system, with specific examples from the codebase. Explain the technology stack including Rust ecosystem components, Tokio async runtime, Axum web framework, and SQLx database access. Address how the modular architecture enables embedding and extending functionality while maintaining clear separation of concerns.

wiki_catalogs.name: Workflow Fundamentals
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content for workflow fundamentals focused on Nebula's DAG-based workflow execution model. Explain the directed acyclic graph structure, node dependencies, and topological ordering principles. Document workflow definition patterns, node types, and execution semantics. Cover workflow validation, versioning, and lifecycle management. Include concrete examples from the actual codebase showing workflow construction, execution flow, and state transitions. Document configuration options, parameters, and return values. Address relationships with other components like the execution engine and storage layer. Explain common implementation patterns for workflow authors and operators. Make content accessible to beginners while providing sufficient technical depth for experienced developers building complex workflows.

Explain the purpose and implementation details of the CLI application with command-line interface, configuration management, and plugin management, and the desktop application with Tauri integration, React UI components, workflow editor, and execution monitoring. Document the Tauri configuration, React component architecture, workflow canvas implementation, and real-time execution visualization. Include concrete examples from the actual codebase showing how to use both applications for workflow development and monitoring. Document command-line options, configuration parameters, UI component APIs, and integration patterns. Explain relationships with core engine and business layer components. Address common use cases, user workflows, and troubleshooting. Make content accessible to beginners while providing sufficient technical depth for experienced developers customizing the desktop interface and extending CLI functionality.

wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content for the Deployment and Operations documentation focused on Nebula's deployment configurations and operational procedures. Explain the purpose and implementation details of Docker deployment with Docker Compose configurations, service orchestration, environment variables, and volume management for both self-hosted and SaaS deployments. Document Kubernetes deployment with Helm charts, service mesh integration, persistent volumes, and monitoring setup. Include concrete examples from the actual deployment configurations showing how to deploy different Nebula variants. Document database migrations, schema evolution, and operational procedures. Explain scaling considerations, backup strategies, and maintenance procedures. Address common deployment scenarios, troubleshooting deployment issues, and operational best practices. Make content accessible to beginners while providing sufficient technical depth for experienced DevOps engineers managing production deployments.

wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content for the Development and Contributing documentation focused on Nebula's development workflow and contribution processes. Explain the purpose and implementation details of the development environment setup, build system with Cargo workspace, testing framework with nextest, and code quality tools. Document the contribution workflow including issue reporting, pull request process, code review guidelines, and continuous integration. Include concrete examples from the actual development configuration showing how to set up the development environment, run tests, and submit contributions. Document coding standards, style guidelines, idiomatic Rust practices, and review checklists. Explain the project's governance model, decision-making process, and community guidelines. Address common development scenarios, debugging techniques, and contribution opportunities. Make content accessible to beginners while providing sufficient technical depth for experienced contributors understanding the project's architecture and development practices.

wiki_catalogs.name: Database Schema Design
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive data model documentation for Nebula's database schema design. Detail entity relationships, field definitions, and data types across the execution management, workflow definitions, credential storage, and resource management tables. Document primary/foreign keys, indexes, and constraints that support workflow execution and persistence. Explain data validation rules, business rules, and referential integrity requirements. Include database schema diagrams showing table relationships and sample data for key entities. Document data access patterns, caching strategies, and performance considerations for workflow execution. Specify data lifecycle, retention policies, and archival rules for executions and audit trails. Include data migration paths, version management, and schema evolution procedures. Address data security, privacy requirements, and access control through database-level permissions. Document the repository pattern implementation and how storage backends (PostgreSQL/SQLite) are abstracted through the persistence layer.

wiki_catalogs.name: API Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create API documentation for Nebula's REST API endpoints and SDK interfaces. For RESTful APIs, document HTTP methods, URL patterns, request/response schemas, and authentication methods for workflow management, execution control, credential operations, and webhook handling. For the SDK API, document the integration author façade including WorkflowBuilder, TestRuntime, action framework APIs, and plugin development interfaces. Include concrete examples showing typical API usage patterns, error handling strategies, and response formats. Document authentication requirements, rate limiting, and security considerations for each endpoint. Explain the relationship between SDK and REST API layers, showing how programmatic access maps to HTTP endpoints. Address common use cases, client implementation guidelines, and performance optimization tips. Include migration guides for API changes and backwards compatibility notes. Document the action macro system and plugin protocol specifications. Provide comprehensive reference material for developers integrating with Nebula programmatically.

wiki_catalogs.name: Troubleshooting and FAQ
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content for the Troubleshooting and FAQ documentation focused on common issues and their solutions when working with Nebula. Explain systematic approaches to diagnosing problems including log analysis, performance profiling, error investigation, and system monitoring. Document common installation problems, runtime errors, performance issues, and integration problems with step-by-step resolution procedures. Include concrete examples from the actual codebase showing how to debug workflow execution failures, credential resolution issues, and plugin communication problems. Document debugging tools, monitoring approaches, and diagnostic techniques. Explain error classification, log interpretation, and telemetry analysis for identifying root causes. Address frequently asked questions about architecture decisions, design trade-offs, and best practices. Make content accessible to beginners encountering issues for the first time while providing sufficient technical depth for experienced developers performing advanced troubleshooting and system optimization.

wiki_overview.content:
<blog>

# Nebula: Workflow Automation Toolkit - Comprehensive Project Overview

## Project Introduction

Nebula is a sophisticated workflow automation toolkit designed as a high-throughput orchestration engine with a first-class integration SDK. The project represents a Rust-native, self-hosted solution that prioritizes reliability, clarity, and developer experience in workflow automation.

### Purpose Statement
Nebula addresses two fundamental failures in common workflow engines:
1. **Integrations are second-class citizens** - Node/connector authoring is an afterthought with poor developer experience
2. **The happy path is assumed** - Real workflows require robust handling of flaky APIs, restarts, and retries

The project's core thesis is to provide a serious orchestration core with honest contracts, where performance and resilience are handled by the runtime while integration developers focus on their core logic.

### Core Goals and Objectives
- **High-throughput workflow execution** with durable state management
- **First-class integration SDK** supporting Rust-native development
- **Reliable execution** that handles concurrent workflows with explicit runtime orchestration
- **Typed boundaries** ensuring integration authors work with validated schemas
- **Self-hosted architecture** giving operators complete control over their infrastructure

### Target Audience
- **Primary**: Developers writing integrations and custom workflow actions
- **Secondary**: Operators deploying and managing workflow automation infrastructure
- **Tertiary**: Organizations seeking a Rust-native, self-hosted workflow automation solution

## Technical Architecture

Nebula employs a modular, layered architecture built around the principle of composability and clear separation of concerns.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CLI Interface] --> B[API Gateway]
    B --> C[Workflow Engine]
    C --> D[Execution Layer]
    C --> E[Credential Management]
    C --> F[Resource Pooling]
    
    D --> G[Storage Backend]
    E --> H[Credential Store]
    F --> I[Resource Manager]
    
    J[Desktop App] --> B
    K[Integration SDK] --> L[Action Framework]
    L --> M[Plugin System]
    
    subgraph "Core Engine"
        C
        D
        E
        F
    end
    
    subgraph "Persistence Layer"
        G
        H
        I
    end
    
    subgraph "Integration Layer"
        L
        M
        K
    end
```

### Component Breakdown

The architecture follows a clear layering pattern:

1. **Presentation Layer**: CLI and Desktop applications
2. **API Layer**: HTTP gateway with authentication and middleware
3. **Orchestration Layer**: Workflow engine with execution control
4. **Integration Layer**: Action framework and plugin system
5. **Persistence Layer**: Storage backends for execution state
6. **Observability Layer**: Metrics, logging, and telemetry

### Design Patterns

Nebula implements several key design patterns:

- **Composition Root Pattern**: Centralized engine initialization and dependency injection
- **Ports & Adapters**: Clean separation between core logic and external dependencies
- **Repository Pattern**: Abstraction over storage backends
- **Observer Pattern**: Event-driven architecture for workflow state changes
- **Strategy Pattern**: Pluggable execution strategies and resource management

### System Relationships

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLI as CLI Client
    participant API as API Gateway
    participant ENGINE as Workflow Engine
    participant STORAGE as Storage Layer
    participant ACTION as Action Runtime
    
    CLI->>+API: Execute Workflow Request
    API->>+ENGINE: Validate & Start Execution
    ENGINE->>+STORAGE: Persist Execution State
    ENGINE->>+ACTION: Dispatch Action Execution
    ACTION-->>-ENGINE: Action Result
    ENGINE->>+STORAGE: Update State & Journal
    ENGINE-->>-API: Execution Progress
    API-->>-CLI: Response with Results
```

## Key Implementation

### Main Entry Points

**CLI Application** (`apps/cli/src/main.rs`)
- Multi-threaded Tokio runtime for async operations
- Comprehensive command dispatch system
- Configurable logging with verbose mode support
- Plugin management and workflow validation capabilities

**API Gateway** (`crates/api/src/lib.rs`)
- Axum-based HTTP server with middleware stack
- JWT and API key authentication
- RFC 9457 compliant error handling
- Webhook endpoint management for trigger actions

**Desktop Application** (`apps/desktop/src/main.tsx`)
- React-based UI with Tauri integration
- Flow-based workflow editor
- Real-time execution monitoring
- Plugin management interface

### Core Modules

**Workflow Engine** (`crates/engine/src/lib.rs`)
- Central orchestrator for workflow execution
- Control queue consumer for durable operations
- Credential resolution and resource management
- Execution state transitions with CAS semantics

**Action Framework** (`crates/action/src/lib.rs`)
- Comprehensive trait family for different action types
- Stateless, stateful, trigger, and resource actions
- Built-in support for pagination and batching
- Webhook and polling trigger implementations

**Storage Layer** (`crates/storage/src/lib.rs`)
- Repository pattern for execution and workflow persistence
- SQLite for development and PostgreSQL for production
- In-memory implementations for testing
- Control queue repository for durable operations

**Resource Management** (`crates/resource/src/lib.rs`)
- Bulkhead pooling for resource lifecycle management
- Health checking and automatic recovery
- RAII-based resource handles
- Support for various topology patterns

### Configuration Approach

**CLI Configuration** (`apps/cli/src/config.rs`)
- Hierarchical configuration loading (defaults → global → local → environment)
- TOML-based configuration with strict validation
- Environment variable support with double-underscore path separator
- Size limits and safety guards against malicious config files

**Deployment Configuration** (`deploy/docker/docker-compose.yml`)
- Multi-service Docker Compose setup
- PostgreSQL for production storage
- Optional Redis caching layer
- Health checks and restart policies

### External Dependencies

The project leverages a carefully curated set of dependencies:

**Core Runtime**: Tokio async runtime with comprehensive feature set
**Serialization**: Serde ecosystem for JSON and structured data
**HTTP**: Axum web framework with Tower middleware
**Database**: SQLx for PostgreSQL/SQLite with async support
**Observability**: OpenTelemetry integration for distributed tracing
**Security**: Argon2 for password hashing, JWT for authentication

### Integration Points

**Plugin System** (`crates/plugin/src/lib.rs`)
- Dynamic plugin loading and registration
- Manifest-based plugin identification
- Capability-based sandboxing
- Cross-plugin dependency management

**Credential Management** (`crates/credential/src/lib.rs`)
- Secure credential storage and rotation
- OAuth2 flow integration
- Secret management with redaction
- Pending credential handling

**Expression Language** (`crates/expression/src/lib.rs`)
- Template-based expression evaluation
- Built-in function library
- Custom function extension points
- Performance-optimized parsing and execution

### Component Relationships

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class WorkflowEngine {
        +execute_workflow()
        +cancel_execution()
        +resume_execution()
    }
    
    class ExecutionRepo {
        +transition()
        +append_journal()
        +save_checkpoint()
    }
    
    class ActionFramework {
        +dispatch_action()
        +handle_result()
        +manage_state()
    }
    
    class CredentialManager {
        +resolve_credential()
        +rotate_credential()
        +store_credential()
    }
    
    class ResourceManager {
        +acquire_resource()
        +release_resource()
        +health_check()
    }
    
    WorkflowEngine --> ExecutionRepo
    WorkflowEngine --> ActionFramework
    WorkflowEngine --> CredentialManager
    WorkflowEngine --> ResourceManager
```

## Key Features

### Functionality Overview

**Workflow Execution Engine**
- DAG-based workflow execution with parallel node processing
- Durable execution state with checkpoint persistence
- Graceful handling of failures and restart scenarios
- Idempotent operation support for reliable processing

**Integration SDK**
- Comprehensive action framework with multiple execution patterns
- Plugin system for extending functionality
- Built-in support for common integration patterns
- Testing harness for action development

**Credential Management**
- Secure credential storage with rotation policies
- OAuth2 flow integration for external services
- Secret redaction in logs and error messages
- Multi-tenant credential isolation

**Resource Pooling**
- Bulkhead pattern for resource management
- Automatic health checking and recovery
- Support for various resource topology patterns
- Performance monitoring and metrics collection

### Implementation Highlights

**Execution Control Queue** (`crates/engine/src/control_consumer.rs`)
- Durable queue consumer for workflow control operations
- Graceful shutdown handling with cleanup procedures
- Backoff strategies for error recovery
- Atomic state transitions with CAS semantics

**Action Execution Pipeline** (`crates/engine/src/credential.rs`)
- Credential resolution and caching
- Resource acquisition and management
- Error classification and retry handling
- Context propagation throughout execution

**Webhook Integration** (`crates/api/src/webhook.rs`)
- Secure webhook endpoint handling
- HMAC signature verification
- Rate limiting and abuse prevention
- Event routing and processing

### Feature Architecture

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialized
    Initialized --> Activated: Workflow Created
    Activated --> Running: Start Executed
    Running --> Completed: All Nodes Finished
    Running --> Failed: Critical Error
    Running --> Paused: Manual Pause
    Paused --> Running: Resume
    Failed --> Resumed: Retry
    Completed --> [*]
    Paused --> Cancelled: Cancel Request
    Cancelled --> [*]
```

The architecture emphasizes reliability through:
- **Durable state persistence** ensuring progress is never lost
- **Atomic operations** preventing inconsistent states
- **Graceful degradation** allowing partial failures without system collapse
- **Comprehensive observability** enabling detailed debugging and monitoring

Nebula represents a mature, production-ready workflow automation platform that balances developer experience with operational reliability, making it suitable for organizations requiring sophisticated workflow automation capabilities.

**Sources:**
- [Cargo.toml](Cargo.toml)
- [main.rs](apps/cli/src/main.rs)
- [lib.rs](crates/engine/src/lib.rs)
- [lib.rs](crates/api/src/lib.rs)
- [lib.rs](crates/sdk/src/lib.rs)
- [main.tsx](apps/desktop/src/main.tsx)
- [lib.rs](crates/action/src/lib.rs)
- [lib.rs](crates/storage/src/lib.rs)
- [lib.rs](crates/resource/src/lib.rs)
- [Cargo.toml](crates/engine/Cargo.toml)
- [PRODUCT_CANON.md](docs/PRODUCT_CANON.md)
- [package.json](apps/desktop/package.json)
- [tauri.conf.json](apps/desktop/src-tauri/tauri.conf.json)
- [config.rs](apps/cli/src/config.rs)
- [docker-compose.yml](deploy/docker/docker-compose.yml)

</blog>
